entries.tutorialSubtitle: Sewing a silk-and-core headband at the spine head and tail
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Headbands are the small banded structures at the top and bottom of a book's spine. In machine-made books they are glued-on strips of cotton tape; in a hand-sewn binding they are worked stitch by stitch over a rolled core, locking each signature to the spine and adding colour.

entries.tutorialTitle: Finding draughts with a smoke pencil
entries.tutorialSubtitle: A room-by-room survey to spot the gaps before you start sealing
entries.tutorialExcerpt: A smoke pencil shows where cold air is leaking into a house by making the airflow visible. A methodical survey on a cold, still day covers skirting boards, window edges, loft hatches, electrical sockets, and pipe entries. About half a day's work, and it costs nothing if you have a candle or a stick of incense.

entries.tutorialTitle: Applying a bonding coat before a skim finish

entries.tutorialTitle: Applying PVA sealer and varnish to air-dry clay
entries.tutorialSubtitle: How to seal a finished air-dry clay piece for durability and light water resistance

entries.tutorialTitle: Applying wax resist for glaze decoration
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Reserving areas of bisqueware from glaze using liquid wax emulsion
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Wax resist is applied to bisqueware before glazing to keep specific areas glaze-free. The wax repels liquid glaze; where wax has been applied, the clay surface stays bare. In the kiln firing, the wax burns away cleanly. The technique is used to keep the foot ring clean, create geometric patterns, or mask areas for two-colour glaze effects.

entries.tutorialTitle: Pulling and attaching a clay handle
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Drawing a strap handle from a clay lump and fixing it to a leather-hard mug or jug
entries.tutorialExcerpt: A pulled handle is drawn from a lump of clay by repeatedly stroking it downward with a wet hand to form a strap. The handle is then bent to shape, scored and slipped at both attachment points, and pressed firmly onto the pot. Pulling produces a stronger handle than extruding because the drawing motion aligns the clay particles along the handle length.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: Why the 6 to 9 mm gap controls everything in a managed hive, and how to keep your equipment working with it.
entries.tutorialExcerpt: The discovery that bees leave a 6 to 9 mm gap clear inside a managed hive (enough to pass through but not enough to fill with comb or propolis) changed beekeeping. entries.tutorialTitle: Buying secondhand: where to find quality items and what to check
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Which product categories reliably deliver secondhand, and the inspection steps that prevent wasted money
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Secondhand is not always a compromise. Cast iron cookware, all-wool knitwear, hardback books, solid timber furniture, and most hand tools are categories where secondhand frequently outperforms new cheap equivalents. The barrier is knowing where to look and what to inspect before buying.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: A gouge-hollowed fruit bowl in seasoned beech
entries.tutorialExcerpt: A round fruit bowl in seasoned beech: 25 cm diameter, 7 cm tall, hollowed with a No. 7 sweep gouge and mallet and finished with walnut oil. The gouge-roughing method used here removes material faster than a hook knife alone and produces a clean floor.

entries.tutorialTitle: Centring half a kilo of stoneware on the wheel
entries.tutorialSubtitle: The foundational move that every thrown piece starts with
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Centring is the act of pushing a mound of clay into a state where it rotates without wobbling under the hands. entries.tutorialSubtitle: Building a tall vessel from stacked and smoothed coils
entries.tutorialExcerpt: A coil-built vase stacks hand-rolled ropes of clay, blending each coil into the wall below to build height gradually. entries.tutorialTitle: Filling gaps in old timber floorboards
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Stopping floor draughts with flexible filler, papier-mache, and strip insert

entries.tutorialTitle: Converting a single-flush toilet to dual flush
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Replacing a siphon mechanism with a dual-flush valve to halve flushing water use

entries.tutorialTitle: Flat roof insulation: warm deck, inverted deck, and cold deck
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Choosing the right approach before you re-cover a flat roof
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Flat roofs fail early when insulation is poorly placed. The warm deck puts insulation above the structural deck and is the correct modern standard. entries.tutorialTitle: Using a Green Cone food digester for cooked food and meat
entries.tutorialSubtitle: How an in-ground digester handles food waste that a standard compost heap cannot
entries.tutorialExcerpt: A Green Cone is a double-walled cone mounted over a basket buried in the ground. It accepts all food waste including cooked food, meat, fish, and dairy that cannot go in a standard compost heap. Solar heating accelerates decomposition; waste is absorbed into the surrounding soil rather than collected as finished compost.

entries.tutorialTitle: Reading green wood: when is it ready to work?
entries.tutorialSubtitle: How to assess a billet's moisture content before starting
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Green wood behaves very differently from seasoned wood: it carves easily, dries and tightens in joints, and changes shape as it dries. This article explains how to read a billet's moisture state before starting work, which species are most tolerant of variable moisture, and what happens when you carve or turn wood that is drier than expected.

entries.tutorialTitle: Treating garden greywater with a simple constructed wetland
entries.tutorialSubtitle: How a shallow planted bed filters laundry and washing-up water for garden reuse
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Greywater from a kitchen or laundry can be diverted to a constructed wetland: a shallow lined bed filled with gravel and planted with wetland species. Plants and the gravel biofilm remove suspended solids, phosphates, and pathogens. The treated water drains to a soakaway or a second storage tank for irrigation.

entries.tutorialTitle: Heat pump flow temperature: why it matters and what to set it to
entries.tutorialSubtitle: The relationship between flow temperature, COP, and radiator sizing
entries.tutorialExcerpt: A heat pump running at 55°C flow temperature operates at a COP of around 2.0-2.5. The same heat pump at 35°C flow operates at COP 3.5-4.5. entries.tutorialSubtitle: Linking letters in formal italic with exit strokes and entry joins
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Formal italic calligraphy is normally written with letters unjoined. This tutorial introduces the joins: the exit stroke angles, the hairline connectors, and the letters that resist joining because their endings are incompatible with flowing entry strokes. The result is a semiformal italic that moves faster and reads more warmly than a fully separated hand.

entries.tutorialTitle: Setting up a kitchen caddy routine for food scraps
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Reducing mess, smell, and fruit flies while keeping a compost-ready caddy on the worktop
entries.tutorialExcerpt: A 5-litre caddy lined with a compostable liner holds 2-3 days of food scraps before it needs emptying. entries.tutorialExcerpt: Kolrosing is a traditional Scandinavian surface decoration: fine lines incised into a carved wooden surface with a knife, then rubbed with dark pigment; traditionally ground charcoal, coffee, or dark soil; to make the lines visible as permanent dark marks. entries.tutorialSubtitle: Dipping a bisque piece in two glazes at different heights to create a colour break and overlap zone
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Two-colour layered dipping applies a base glaze all over, then partially dips in a second contrasting glaze that overlaps the first to a set depth. entries.tutorialSubtitle: Pulling sheets by hand using lokta fibre in the Nepalese tradition
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Lokta paper is made from the bark fibres of Daphne papyracea, a shrub native to the Himalayan foothills. It is among the world's most durable hand-formed papers: the long interlocking fibres resist tearing, hold a sharp pen line, and age without brittleness. This tutorial forms small sheets using pre-prepared dry lokta fibre strips.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: The two liquid clay mixtures hand-builders rely on for joining and decorating

entries.tutorialSubtitle: Aerated and non-aerated methods for turning finished compost into liquid feed

entries.tutorialTitle: Making a mini swale to slow and sink rainwater
entries.tutorialSubtitle: A simple on-contour trench that turns runoff into ground infiltration
entries.tutorialExcerpt: A swale is a level trench dug on the contour of a slope. Rainwater running down the slope fills the trench and infiltrates into the ground rather than washing away. A mini swale 200-300 mm wide and 150 mm deep is enough to noticeably reduce runoff from a garden slope and recharge soil moisture during heavy rain.

entries.tutorialTitle: Mishima line inlay on air-dry clay
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Incised lines filled with coloured slip for a two-tone inlaid surface
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Mishima is a surface decoration technique in which lines are incised into leather-hard clay and then filled with slip of a contrasting colour. Once dry, the excess slip is scraped back to reveal the lines flush with the surrounding surface. This version adapts the technique for air-dry clay using acrylic-tinted slip.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: Producing an optical wave interference pattern by crossing two fine comb passes
entries.tutorialExcerpt: The moiré pattern in marbling comes from two fine-combed passes at 90° to each other with very closely spaced teeth. Where the two grid patterns cross, they produce an interference effect that creates the characteristic circular or wavy optical illusion. It is among the most technically demanding of the standard carrageenan marbling patterns.

entries.tutorialTitle: Opening a thrown mound on the wheel
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Pressing the first hollow into a centred mound without breaking the base
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Opening is the step that follows centring: pressing thumbs down through the top of the mound to create the initial hollow and set the base thickness. entries.tutorialSubtitle: Producing the curved fan-like peacock design on a carrageenan size
entries.tutorialExcerpt: The peacock pattern is one of the most recognisable designs in the Western marbling tradition. It begins as a simple nonpareil (fine-combed stripe) and is then converted into arching fan shapes by drawing a wide-toothed rake through the stripes in a series of curves. This tutorial covers the sequence from colour laying to raking to lifting the sheet.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: Adding paper pockets, fold-out flaps, and tucked inserts to journal pages
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Pockets and fold-out flaps turn journal pages from flat surfaces into storage systems. A pocket inside the back cover holds loose notes; a gusseted fold-out extends a page to three times its width; a tucked insert allows a half-page to drop down. entries.tutorialSubtitle: Mounting, tooling, and cutting on a reciprocating pole lathe
entries.tutorialExcerpt: The pole lathe turns a blank by a reciprocating spring-pole drive cord; the blank rotates one direction on the downstroke, reverses on the up. Cutting happens on the downstroke only. This technique covers blank mounting, roughing with the gouge, smoothing with the skew chisel, and parting off; the four operations that produce a finished turned component.

entries.tutorialTitle: Setting up the pole lathe: drive centre and dead centre
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Mounting a billet correctly between the drive and dead centres
entries.tutorialExcerpt: The drive centre and dead centre are the two points that hold the billet on the pole lathe. Getting the mounting right; centred on the billet axis, drive centre fully engaged, dead centre lubricated; determines the quality of every turning that follows. This technique guide covers centre-finding, mounting, and the first two cuts to confirm the billet is running true.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: Cutting and layering symmetrical folk paper designs in the Łowicz tradition
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Wycinanki (pronounced vee-chee-nahn-kee) is the Polish folk art of paper-cutting, most elaborately developed in the Łowicz region where multiple layers of coloured tissue or craft paper are cut and layered to build up floral and zoomorphic designs. The cuts are made with scissors alone, no knife, producing the characteristic rounded cuts of the hand tradition.

entries.tutorialTitle: Post and rail fencing for stock
entries.tutorialSubtitle: How to set posts and fix rails to build a fence that holds cattle, horses, and large livestock.

entries.tutorialTitle: Pulling a cylinder wall on the wheel
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Drawing clay upward from an opened floor to create an even-walled cylinder
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Pulling is the motion that raises clay from the floor of an opened mound into a wall. One hand works inside the hollow, one outside; together they compress and lift the clay upward in a slow coordinated movement. The cylinder is the base form behind every thrown piece: bowls, mugs, and vases all start here.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: Applying watercolour washes over burned wood for a mixed-media effect
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Colour washes applied over pyrography burns combine the precision of burned line work with the warmth of pigment. The burned lines resist the watercolour wash, creating a natural resist effect. This technique covers wash preparation, application sequence, and how to maintain the tonal structure of the pyrography beneath the colour.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: A tonal dog portrait burned on seasoned birch, worked from a reference photograph
entries.tutorialExcerpt: A tonal dog portrait burned on a 200 mm by 250 mm seasoned birch panel, worked from a close-cropped reference photograph. The project covers grid-based design transfer, fur-stroke technique, value range building, and fine-detail work on the nose and eyes. This is an advanced pyrography project requiring a variable-temperature nichrome-wire burner.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: A chevron border and central motif burned on a basswood panel
entries.tutorialExcerpt: A geometric pyrography panel on basswood: a chevron border around the perimeter and a simple eight-pointed star at the centre. Geometric patterns are the ideal introduction to pyrography; they teach consistent line weight and temperature control without requiring freehand drawing skill. The design is marked out in pencil with ruler and compass before burning.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: Building tonal gradients for animal and figure portraits in pyrography
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Portrait pyrography differs from geometric or line work in one way: tone does everything and line does almost nothing. This technique covers the layer-by-layer approach to building tonal gradients; starting with the lightest areas, building darkest accents last; and the specific tip movements that produce fur, feather, and skin texture on wood.

entries.tutorialTitle: Marking and clipping a queen bee
entries.tutorialSubtitle: How to spot, catch, mark, and optionally clip your queen before returning her to the colony.
entries.tutorialExcerpt: A marked queen is a known queen. One small coloured dot on her thorax tells you at a glance which year she was introduced, whether this is still the queen you started with, and whether she survived the winter. The clipping step is optional but worth understanding.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: What the symbols on packaging mean, and what actually gets collected
entries.tutorialExcerpt: The widely recycled, check locally, and not currently recycled labels on UK packaging indicate national collection rates. Above 75% of local authorities collect a material: widely recycled. 20-75%: check locally. Below 20%: not currently recycled. The label tells you about collection infrastructure, not about whether the material is technically recyclable.

  Subtitle: What the symbols on packaging mean, and what actually gets collected
  Excerpt: The widely recycled, check locally, and not currently recycled labels on UK packaging indicate national collection rates. Above 75% of local authorities collect a material: widely recycled. 20-75%: check locally. Below 20%: not currently re
  Category: sustainability / waste-reduction
  Candidate source: pexels

entries.tutorialTitle: Repairing electronics instead of replacing: what is feasible and how to start
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Battery replacements, screen repairs, and the iFixit-first approach to common faults
entries.tutorialExcerpt: From October 2021, UK regulations require manufacturers of washing machines, dishwashers, TVs, and lighting to make spare parts available for 7-10 years. Smartphones and laptops are not yet in scope, but a thriving market in parts and repair guides means screen replacements, battery swaps, and charging port repairs are within reach for most people.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: The L-shaped combustion chamber, insulated burn tunnel, and why it outperforms an open fire

entries.tutorialSubtitle: A round flat-based bread basket worked in the coiled rush tradition
entries.tutorialExcerpt: A coiled rush bread basket: 25 cm diameter, 8 cm sides, a flat base worked in concentric coils of bound rush. Coiling is an older and simpler construction method than willow stake-and-strand; the only tools required are a bodkin and a knife. entries.tutorialExcerpt: A plaited rush mat in the checkerboard over-under weave: 45 cm long and 30 cm wide, using soaked bulrush. entries.tutorialSubtitle: Writing the compressed display capitals of the Roman rustic hand
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Rustica is a compressed, slightly condensed form of Roman capitals used for headings and emphasis in classical manuscripts. Its characteristic features are a nib angle near vertical, tightly compressed letterforms, and small diagonal serifs. It pairs well with a broader-spaced Foundational or Italic minuscule body text.

entries.tutorialTitle: Reducing shower water use without reducing shower quality
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Aerated showerheads, flow restrictors, and shower timers compared
entries.tutorialExcerpt: A standard shower head flows at 10-15 litres per minute. entries.tutorialTitle: Detecting slow water leaks in a house
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Using the water meter, dye tablets, and visual checks to find drips and seeps
entries.tutorialExcerpt: A dripping tap loses around 5,500 litres per year. A running toilet loses 200-400 litres per day. Neither are immediately obvious. Reading the water meter with all taps off, overnight, is the fastest way to confirm whether a leak exists. Dye tablets identify toilet cistern leaks without dismantling anything.

entries.tutorialTitle: Assessing shading losses before installing solar PV
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Horizon surveys, pitch and orientation, and PVGIS as a planning tool
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Shading from a neighbouring chimney that covers one row of panels for two hours in December can reduce annual yield by 5-15% depending on inverter type. A horizon survey with a compass and inclinometer, combined with the PVGIS online tool, gives a good annual yield estimate before you commit.

entries.tutorialTitle: Drying green-wood spoon blanks without cracking
entries.tutorialSubtitle: How moisture leaves a carved spoon and what to do about it
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Green-wood spoon blanks crack because moisture leaves unevenly. This article explains why cracking happens, how to dry blanks slowly and safely, what to do when a crack appears, and how to read the signs that a blank is dry enough to finish. Applies to all spoon-carving species.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: What to do when a colony is running short of stores before the spring flow arrives.
entries.tutorialExcerpt: April is a dangerous month for bees. The colony has been growing since February, stores have been consumed faster than in January, and the early flowers have not yet opened reliably. A colony that felt heavy in November can be light enough to starve by late March.

entries.tutorialTitle: Transferring bees from a nuc to a full hive
entries.tutorialSubtitle: How to move a five-frame nucleus colony into a full-size national brood box.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: The British tradition of small wooden household objects and why it still matters
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Treen; from the Old English for 'of trees'; is the collective name for the small wooden objects that formed the domestic material culture of Britain from the medieval period onward: spoons, bowls, trenchers, measures, salt boxes, butter moulds, and candle holders. This primer covers the tradition, the species that defined it, and how the pieces in this category connect to it.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: What turning does, when to do it, and how to check the heap is actually hot
entries.tutorialExcerpt: A hot compost heap reaches 55-70°C in the first week if it is correctly constructed. Turning moves the cool outer material into the hot centre, aerates the heap to restore oxygen for microbial activity, and extends the hot phase. A compost thermometer tells you when to turn and when the heap has finished its active phase.

entries.tutorialTitle: Building a two-bay compost bin from pallets or timber
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Why two bays outperform one, and how to size and build them
entries.tutorialExcerpt: A single bin fills up and cannot be turned. Two bays (one active, one maturing) let you fill one side, then fork the contents into the second bay to finish while the first fills again. Each bay needs roughly 1 cubic metre of volume to generate enough heat for active decomposition.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: Writing the rounded single-case hand of early Christian manuscripts
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Uncial is a single-case script, it has no separate uppercase or lowercase, that was the dominant book hand of early medieval Europe from the 4th to the 8th century. entries.tutorialExcerpt: Endpapers are the leaves that bridge the text block to the boards in a case-bound book. entries.tutorialSubtitle: Why elevation matters and how to build a stable stand from timber or blocks

entries.tutorialSubtitle: A bent willow rod wreath in a heart form for seasonal decoration
entries.tutorialExcerpt: A willow heart wreath takes one bundle of fresh or soaked willow rods and 30 minutes: bend the rods into a heart shape, bind them together, and add a hanging loop. entries.tutorialSubtitle: Oval willow laundry basket in the English country tradition
entries.tutorialExcerpt: A large oval willow laundry basket: 55 cm long, 40 cm wide, 35 cm deep, with a fixed base and hinged lid. entries.tutorialExcerpt: A round willow log basket: 40 cm diameter base, 30 cm sides, handle arches at each end. entries.tutorialSubtitle: Setting up and weaving the slath base for a round willow basket
entries.tutorialExcerpt: The round base is the foundation of most willow baskets: a cross of thick sticks bound at the centre and opened out, then paired off with finer weavers until the base is the right diameter. entries.tutorialSubtitle: Double versus triple glazing, frame types, and BFRC ratings explained

entries.tutorialSubtitle: How to monitor a colony through winter without opening the hive.
entries.tutorialExcerpt: You do not open a beehive in winter. But you do check it. A short walk to the apiary once a fortnight tells you whether the colony is alive, whether stores are running low, and whether the entrance is blocked. This is what to do and what to do if something looks wrong.

entries.tutorialSubtitle: How to keep outdoor pigs warm and dry through a cold, wet winter.
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Pigs tolerate cold better than heat, but they cannot maintain body temperature on wet ground with no shelter. A deep, dry bed inside a well-positioned arc is the simplest and most effective way to keep outdoor pigs comfortable from November to March.

entries.tutorialTitle: Carving a wood spirit in relief
entries.tutorialSubtitle: A face emerging from the grain: low-relief wood spirit technique
entries.tutorialExcerpt: A wood spirit is a carved face emerging from a log end or slab; a tradition from Scandinavian and Eastern European folk carving. This technique guide covers laying out the face in low relief, the sequence of gouge and knife cuts, grain reading for the curved surfaces, and finishing without obscuring the tool marks.

entries.tutorialTitle: Worm egg counts and wormer resistance
entries.tutorialSubtitle: Why routine worming damages your flock long-term, and how targeted treatment works instead.
entries.tutorialExcerpt: Blanket worming of all sheep on a fixed schedule was standard practice for decades. It is now understood to be one of the main drivers of anthelmintic resistance. Faecal egg counts take the guesswork out of treatment decisions and protect the wormers you still have.
